skill.name: gitops-workflow
skill.description: Build GitOps workflows with Argo CD and Flux for declarative Kubernetes deployments, automated sync policies, secret handling, and progressive delivery.
skill.summary: Guide Kubernetes teams through GitOps setup with Argo CD, Flux, sync policies, and safe deployment practices.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command-execution and weak-crypto patterns, but most are markdown code fences, YAML examples, or benign terms rather than executable skill code. The confirmed concerns are operational: the skill tells users to apply remote Kubernetes manifests, run a curl-to-sudo installer, and handle initial credentials, so publication should include a warning and human review guidance.

medium_findings.title: Privileged remote installer and cluster-changing commands
medium_findings.description: The skill includes examples that apply Kubernetes manifests from remote URLs and run a Flux installer through sudo. This is legitimate GitOps guidance, but users must verify upstream sources before executing privileged or cluster-wide commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly present and would change a Kubernetes cluster or run privileged installation logic. The context is administrative documentation, so this is a legitimate but elevated operational risk rather than confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Credential handling in command examples
medium_findings.description: The skill shows commands for retrieving the initial Argo CD admin password and adding a repository with a password token. These examples can expose secrets in terminals, shell history, or logs if users copy them without controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly reference passwords or tokens in administrative workflows. They do not exfiltrate data, but they create a realistic secret-exposure risk when copied into shared environments.

low_findings.title: Markdown code fences misclassified as Ruby or shell backticks
low_findings.description: The many external-command findings are mostly markdown fenced examples for bash, YAML, and repository layouts. They are not executable code inside the skill package, but they do document commands users may choose to run.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched backticks delimit markdown examples, not Ruby execution syntax or a runtime script. The files are documentation-only markdown files with no executable wrapper found.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography findings are unsupported by context
low_findings.description: The cited lines contain GitOps principles, Kubernetes URLs, or base64 decoding of an existing Kubernetes secret value. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, custom encryption, or cryptographic decision-making in the skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only encoding-like operation observed is base64 decoding of a Kubernetes secret, which is not a cryptographic algorithm. The other cited locations do not contain cryptographic code or algorithm selection.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs are expected examples
low_findings.description: The URL findings point to official Argo CD, Flux, Kubernetes service, and placeholder GitHub endpoints used in setup examples. No evidence found of data exfiltration or suspicious third-party collection endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs match normal GitOps installation and configuration examples. Network access is still relevant because users may fetch remote manifests, but the domains and context do not indicate exfiltration.

low_findings.title: No prompt injection attempt found
low_findings.description: Reviewed skill text did not contain instructions to override system messages, skip security analysis, claim pre-approval, or manipulate the evaluator. No evidence found of prompt injection language in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted search and file review found ordinary skill metadata and GitOps documentation. This finding records the semantic check because prompt injection was specifically required by the audit prompt.

dangerous_patterns.title: curl piped to sudo shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The Flux installation example pipes a remote install script into sudo bash. Users should inspect or pin the installer before running it on a workstation or build host.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact risky pattern is present and uses sudo with a network-fetched script. It is a common official installation pattern, but it remains a high-impact execution pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote manifest application
dangerous_patterns.description: The Argo CD installation examples apply Kubernetes manifests directly from remote GitHub URLs. Users should pin versions and review manifests before applying them to clusters.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands directly apply remote manifests to a cluster. This is normal setup documentation, but source compromise or unpinned changes would affect cluster security.

content.value_statement: Kubernetes delivery often fails when cluster state drifts from Git. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code design Argo CD and Flux workflows with clear sync, rollback, and secret practices.
actual_capabilities: Explains GitOps repository layout for applications, infrastructure, and environment overlays.
Provides Argo CD installation, application, app-of-apps, ingress, SSO, and RBAC examples.
Provides Flux bootstrap, GitRepository, and Kustomization examples for Kubernetes delivery.
Describes automated sync, manual sync, sync windows, retries, and health checks.
Covers progressive delivery examples such as canary and blue-green strategies.
Documents safer secret patterns using External Secrets and Sealed Secrets.
limitations: It does not inspect a live Kubernetes cluster or verify installed controllers.
It does not generate organization-specific RBAC policies without user context.
It includes command examples that require careful review before execution.
It does not replace platform security, release approval, or incident response processes.

use_cases.title: Set up a GitOps foundation
use_cases.description: Create a repository structure and controller setup for declarative Kubernetes delivery.

use_cases.title: Standardize deployment sync behavior
use_cases.description: Define auto-sync, manual sync, retry, prune, and maintenance window policies across environments.

use_cases.title: Improve release safety
use_cases.description: Add approval gates, progressive delivery, rollback practices, and secret handling guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a basic GitOps setup
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a first Kubernetes GitOps rollout.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the gitops-workflow skill to design a simple Argo CD setup for one Kubernetes cluster and two environments.

prompt_templates.title: Create sync policies
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate policy design for staging and production.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the gitops-workflow skill to propose Argo CD and Flux sync policies for staging auto-sync and production manual approval.

prompt_templates.title: Review an existing repository layout
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of GitOps repository organization.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the gitops-workflow skill to review this repository layout and suggest environment, application, and infrastructure improvements.

prompt_templates.title: Design a multi-cluster release model
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced architecture for regulated or large environments.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the gitops-workflow skill to design a multi-cluster GitOps workflow with promotion gates, rollback paths, sync windows, and secret management.

output_examples.input: Plan GitOps for a staging and production Kubernetes service.
output: A repository layout separating applications, infrastructure, and environment overlays.
Recommended Argo CD or Flux controller setup steps.
Sync policy guidance for staging automation and production approvals.

output_examples.input: Review our production sync policy before enabling auto-sync.
output: A risk review of prune, self-heal, retry, and sync window settings.
Recommendations for approvals, rollback testing, and alerting.
Warnings for settings that may delete or overwrite production resources.

output_examples.input: Add secret management guidance to a GitOps design.
output: A comparison of External Secrets and Sealed Secrets usage patterns.
Guidance to avoid plain secrets in Git.
Operational checks for rotation, access control, and audit logging.

best_practices: Pin remote manifests and installation versions before applying them to clusters.
Use manual approvals, sync windows, and tested rollback paths for production.
Keep secrets out of Git and use external or sealed secret workflows.
anti_patterns: Do not enable production auto-sync with prune before testing failure modes.
Do not paste tokens or initial passwords into shared terminals or logs.
Do not apply remote install scripts without source review or version pinning.

faq.question: What does this skill help automate?
faq.answer: It helps design GitOps workflows for Kubernetes using Argo CD, Flux, declarative repositories, and sync policies.

faq.question: Can it choose between Argo CD and Flux?
faq.answer: It can compare both approaches and suggest a fit based on cluster access, team workflow, and delivery needs.

faq.question: Does it run Kubernetes commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Users must review commands before running them in their own environment.

faq.question: Is it safe for production planning?
faq.answer: It can support production planning, but production changes need approval gates, testing, monitoring, and security review.

faq.question: How does it handle secrets?
faq.answer: It recommends keeping secrets out of Git and using tools such as External Secrets or Sealed Secrets.

faq.question: Which AI tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
